notes: Page is unambiguously our Jackie McLean (1931-2006, alto saxophonist). birthYear/deathYear/instrument all match. Bio claims verified: distinctive tone ('bittersweet', 'piercing', 'searing'), bebop/hard-bop start (tutored by Monk, Powell, Parker; early recordings 'in the hard bop school'), later embrace of the avant-garde (Let Freedom Ring, 1962, began his avant-garde period). 'Distinctive cry' and 'emotional directness' are editorial.

Dates match (Dec 1, 1951 - Sep 21, 1987). Bio claims verified: fretless bass, harmonics, lyrical solos ('fretless bass, lyrical solos, bass chords and innovative use of harmonics'), Weather Report membership 1976-1981, solo albums, early death at 35 from injuries after a beating. 'Revolutionized', 'changed music forever', 'unique genius' are editorial; evidence supports the stature claims ('widely regarded as one of the greatest and most influential bassists of all time').

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: Willie James "Blood" Ulmer (February 8, 1940 – June 3, 2026) was an American jazz, free funk and blues guitarist and singer.
Ulmer died on June 3, 2026, at the age of 86.

notes: Right entity. Two field errors: birth year is 1940 per evidence, not 1942; and the page records his death on June 3, 2026 (ten days before today), so deathYear null and the present-tense bio ('is a guitarist and vocalist') are outdated -- the dataset almost certainly predates the death, but it now needs updating. Bio content otherwise verified: Ornette Coleman association and harmolodic link (first electric guitarist to record/tour extensively with Coleman; album 'Harmolodic Guitar with Strings'), and both named albums are in the discography: Tales of Captain Black (Artists House, 1979), Are You Glad to Be in America? (Rough Trade, 1980).

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: He has been prominent as a performer and recording artist on the jazz scene since the late 1980s, focusing on saxophones, flute and clarinets.

notes: Right entity; bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified (born Jan 3, 1969; virtuosity on saxophones and woodwinds; cousin of Regina Carter). The instruments field is corrupted: a single token 'bass clarinetsaxophonesflutes' with lost delimiters. This is a formatting/data-corruption defect, not a wrong-entity or wrong-fact problem -- the intended instruments (bass clarinet, saxophones, flutes) are consistent with the evidence ('saxophones, flute and clarinets'; bass clarinet specifically is not named but 'clarinets' plural is compatible). Repair: split into a proper array.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; every claim verified: born 1995, Houston upbringing, move to New York (New School), debut as leader on Blue Note in 2018 (Flight). Instruments piano/keyboards match 'jazz pianist, keyboardist'.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: dates (Jan 29, 1936 - Aug 2, 1983), uncredited Motown bassist of the 1960s/early 1970s, Rock and Roll Hall of Fame 2000, 23 Billboard Hot 100 number-ones and 56 R&B number-ones as a session musician. Note for editors: he is a Motown session bassist with jazz roots rather than a jazz artist per se, but that is a curation choice, not a factual error.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: dates (Feb 1, 1894 - Nov 17, 1955), stride piano pioneer, key figure in ragtime-to-jazz evolution alongside Jelly Roll Morton, influence on Basie/Ellington/Tatum/Monk, and Fats Waller as his student.

Right entity; born 4 March 1947, Norwegian saxophonist, no death. Bio claims verified: free-jazz background ('By 1973 he had turned his back on the harsh dissonances of avant-garde jazz'), Gregorian-chant connection (Officium with the Hilliard Ensemble, 'one of ECM's biggest-selling albums of all time'), strong ECM association. Tone descriptions are editorial but echo the evidence ('sharp-edged tone, long, keening, sustained notes'). One gap: the extract says only 'saxophonist' -- it never names tenor or soprano specifically, so the species-level instruments field is confirmed at genus level only.
Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead sentence; verified: born January 21, 1975, jazz pianist, composer, educator, multimedia/theatrical installation work (IN MY MIND, museum commissions).

fieldIssues.evidenceQuote: The group was formed in the mid-1950s when Brand and Mackay Davashe began working together initiated a collaboration with Gwangwa and Masekela.

notes: Right entity. Low-confidence flag: if birthYear means formation year, the evidence says 'formed in the mid-1950s', contradicting 1959 -- but that evidence sentence is itself garbled and may describe a precursor collaboration; 1959 is the year they recorded Jazz Epistle, Verse 1, and the page's '56-year hiatus' before the 2016 reunion implies activity ended 1960. Human should decide the intended semantics. Not flagged: 'recorded only one album' (consistent: recorded the album 'just before breaking up'); 'Sharpeville massacre ended their brief existence' is a simplification -- evidence has them breaking up around the 1959 recording, yet also ties the members' dispersal to post-Sharpeville exile; 'South Africa's first bebop group' vs evidence 'first important (albeit short-lived) bebop band' is a minor wording gap. Featuring Abdullah Ibrahim (Dollar Brand) and Hugh Masekela verified. Instruments list omits Jonas Gwangwa's trombone (omission, not contradiction).

Right entity; dates match (Jan 29, 1939 - Oct 25, 2000). 'The Newest Sound Around' with Ran Blake verified as her first record, released 1962 (RCA Victor; recorded 1961). 'Poet' verified ('jazz singer, poet and composer'); 'dancer' supported (studied dance at Bard, created choreography, her ensemble fused poetry, music and dance). Collaborations with free improvisers across Europe and America verified (Gunter Hampel, Carla Bley, Braxton, Shepp, Waldron). 'Landmark', 'expanded the boundaries', 'influencing generations' are editorial.

Right entity. Bio verified: 'jazz's first great arranger' matches 'Morton was jazz's first arranger, proving that a genre rooted in improvisation could retain its essential characteristics when notated'; Red Hot Peppers recordings for Victor from 1926 (late 1920s) verified. Caution on birthYear: the evidence is internally inconsistent -- the lead says 'c. September 20, 1890' (matches ours), but the shortDescription says '(1885-1941)' and the biography text says born 'around 1885' (he claimed 1884). Ours matches the page's primary lead date, so not flagged as an error, but a reviewer may want 'c. 1890'. deathYear 1941 verified.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: Gerald Stenhouse Jemmott, born March 22, 1946, bass guitarist, chief session bassist of late 1960s/early 1970s soul, blues, and jazz dates, two Grammy Awards.

Right entity; dates match (Dec 4, 1930 - Dec 10, 2013). Verified: Bill Evans partnership ('formed a studio partnership with Bill Evans... appearing on five albums with Evans from 1962 to 1966' -- 'duo recordings' specifically is a fair gloss, supported by the partnership framing), Sonny Rollins (1961-62, 1964), Art Farmer (1962-64), Paul Desmond (six albums as featured soloist; Concierto). 'Influenced virtually every jazz guitarist who followed' and 'subtle, restrained' are editorial.

Right entity; dates match (Dec 8, 1928 - Feb 8, 2005). Verified: Hammond B-3 popularizer, bebop-plus-blues style ('combined bluesy licks with bebop-based single note runs'), prolific Blue Note run from 1956 (about 40 sessions in eight years, so late 1950s and 1960s holds), influence on a generation of organists (McGriff, McDuff, Holmes, DeFrancesco...). Caution for repair-writer: 'single-handedly brought the Hammond B-3 organ into jazz' is editorial hyperbole -- evidence notes the organ was already used in jazz by Fats Waller, Count Basie, and Wild Bill Davis, and that Smith 'helped to popularize' it. Treated as opinion, not a factual error, but the wording could be softened.

The stored Wikipedia URL returned no page (page: null), so nothing could be entity-matched or fact-checked: birthYear 1933, deathYear 2016, saxophone, Soul Media, the 1970s jazz-funk framing, and the 'Funky Stuff' album are all unverified. Judge's background knowledge (not citable) raises doubt about deathYear 2016 -- Inagaki may have remained active after that date -- so prioritize finding a working source for the death year specifically. The wikiLinkVerdict is unsure because we cannot see what the stored URL resolves to.

Right entity; no contradictions. Verified: dates (15 July 1928 - 2 January 1973), Kingston, Jamaica birth, alto saxophone, free-form pioneer in Britain, albums Free Form (Jazzland, 1960) and Abstract (Columbia UK, 1962), and the Ornette Coleman comparison ('often compared to Ornette Coleman's roughly contemporary breakthrough in the United States'). Unverifiable substantive claims: (1) 'the two were unaware of each other's work' -- the evidence neither supports nor denies mutual unawareness, and it is a strong claim; (2) 'Indo-Jazz Suites with violinist John Mayer' -- the extract truncates before any mention of John Mayer or the Indo-Jazz works; the lead's 'global music fusion' supports the direction only. 'Preceded many American free jazz recordings' and 'far less recognition' are loose/editorial (the latter is broadly supported by the noted hostility of the British jazz establishment).

Right entity; no contradictions. Verified: dates (April 24, 1937 - June 30, 2001), tenor saxophone, 1960s Blue Note era as leader and sideman (nearly 30 albums, 1963-1968), Kenny Dorham association, Horace Silver ('Song for My Father'), Andrew Hill (Black Fire, Point of Departure), and the 1991 Verve signing. Unverifiable substantive claims: the Grammys for his tributes to Billy Strayhorn and Miles Davis -- the extract truncates right after the Verve signing and never reaches the 1990s albums or awards. 'Smoky, distinctive sound' and 'inexorable logic' are editorial.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified: born December 29, 1952, multi-instrumentalist best known on tenor, recorded on alto clarinet, flute and drums, Grammy Award, Down Beat poll mentions, wife Judi Silvano, longtime Paul Motian trio with Bill Frisell. One gap: 'tarogato' in the instruments field is not named in the extract -- the lead's 'amongst other instruments' leaves it consistent but unconfirmed.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: dates (January 13, 1929 - May 23, 1994), recorded and performed with Oscar Peterson, Duke Ellington, and Ella Fitzgerald (six Pablo albums with Fitzgerald), solo guitar renown via Virtuoso (Pablo, 1974).

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead and fully verified: born 1995, Chicago-raised vibraphonist living in Brooklyn, started on drums at his father's church, high school (Chicago High School for the Arts) and college (New School) education, signed with Blue Note (KingMaker, 2019). This was a known past collision target (Diana Ross facts), but the current content is about the correct Joel Ross -- no trace of the old contamination.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead; all claims verified: born February 27, 1965, pianist, brother of Gene Calderazzo, extensive work in Michael Brecker's quintet (from 1987) and the Branford Marsalis Quartet (succeeding Kenny Kirkland in 1998), own bands as leader.

Right entity; no contradictions, but the extract truncates around 1957, leaving several substantive claims uncheckable: 'Giant Steps' and 'My Favorite Things' as albums, the 'sheets of sound' coinage, the classic quartet personnel (McCoy Tyner, Jimmy Garrison, Elvin Jones), liver cancer as cause of death, and soprano saxophone (only tenor is explicit: 'switched to playing tenor saxophone in 1947'). Verified: dates (Sept 23, 1926 - July 17, 1967; age 40 at death is consistent with the dates), hard bop start and the Miles Davis / Thelonious Monk association ('1955-1957: Miles and Monk period'), modal pioneering, forefront of free jazz, spiritual dimension, and A Love Supreme as his most acclaimed album. The unverified claims are standard Coltrane biography, but the pack evidence cannot confirm them.

Right entity; no contradictions. Verified: born 4 January 1942, guitarist, played on Miles Davis's In a Silent Way and Bitches Brew before forming the Mahavishnu Orchestra ('His 1970s electric band'), fusion of jazz/rock with Indian and classical influences, Shakti founded 1974 as a seminal Indian-jazz fusion band. Unverifiable substantive claims: Mahavishnu formed specifically 'in 1971' (evidence gives no formation year), 'devoted practitioner of Sri Chinmoy's teachings' (Sri Chinmoy is never mentioned in the extract; the 'Mahavishnu' alias is consistent but not probative), and influence 'from Al Di Meola to progressive metal players' (Di Meola absent from the extract). 'Spiritually charged', 'lightning-fast' are editorial; his spiritual orientation generally is supported.

Bio is verbatim the Wikipedia lead plus opening biography sentences; all claims verified: born December 22, 1959, Brooklyn, electric bass at 10, performing/composing at 12, acoustic bass at 15, piano by 16, R&B radio and grandfather's jazz records. Instruments double bass / bass guitar verified ('Patitucci switches between double bass and electric bass').
